skill.name: hybrid-cloud-architect
skill.description: Guides hybrid and multi-cloud architecture across AWS, Azure, Google Cloud, OpenStack, VMware, Kubernetes, and edge environments.
skill.summary: Plan secure, cost-aware hybrid cloud architectures across public, private, and edge platforms.
skill.author: sickn33

security_audit.summary: Static analysis reported many high-risk keywords, but review found they are false positives caused by cloud architecture terminology. The backtick finding is Markdown formatting for a file name, and the sensitive, C2, ransomware, weak-crypto, and reconnaissance matches appear in defensive or infrastructure planning context.

low_findings.title: False Positive: Markdown Backticks Misread as Shell Execution
low_findings.description: Line 28 uses Markdown backticks around resources/implementation-playbook.md and instructs the assistant to open a reference file. No shell command, Ruby execution, or command substitution is present.
low_findings.confidence_reasoning: The line contains only prose and a Markdown-formatted file path. There is no executable syntax or user-controlled command invocation.

low_findings.title: False Positive: Security Keywords Used Defensively
low_findings.description: Lines 73, 76, and 116 mention SAML, compliance frameworks, and ransomware protection as part of hybrid cloud security planning. These are defensive architecture topics, not credential theft, command and control, or ransomware behavior.
low_findings.confidence_reasoning: The surrounding section is titled Hybrid Security & Compliance and Disaster Recovery & Business Continuity. The terms are used for legitimate defensive controls.

low_findings.title: False Positive: Reconnaissance and Weak-Crypto Matches in Cloud Terminology
low_findings.description: The repeated low and high severity keyword matches occur in ordinary cloud architecture text such as design, discovery, disaster recovery, monitoring, platforms, and example prompts. No code, scripts, scanning commands, or cryptographic implementation are present.
low_findings.confidence_reasoning: The file is a prose-only skill description with no executable implementation. The matched words are part of architecture guidance rather than offensive operations.

content.value_statement: Hybrid cloud planning is hard when teams must balance cost, compliance, latency, and platform fit. This skill helps structure architecture decisions across public cloud, private cloud, Kubernetes, and edge environments.
actual_capabilities: Compares workload placement across AWS, Azure, Google Cloud, OpenStack, VMware, Kubernetes, and edge platforms.
Plans hybrid connectivity using dedicated links, VPN, SD-WAN, routing, DNS, and load balancing patterns.
Maps architecture choices to compliance, data sovereignty, identity, encryption, and security monitoring needs.
Creates migration, modernization, and disaster recovery strategies with phased rollout considerations.
Supports cost analysis, right-sizing, FinOps governance, chargeback models, and budget monitoring.
Recommends Infrastructure as Code and operations practices for consistent multi-cloud management.
limitations: It provides architecture guidance, not live cloud account inspection or deployment execution.
It cannot verify current provider pricing, quotas, or service availability without external research.
It does not replace formal security, compliance, legal, or procurement review.
It may need detailed workload, network, data, and regulatory inputs for precise recommendations.

use_cases.title: Plan a Regulated Hybrid Cloud
use_cases.description: Design a target architecture that balances compliance, data placement, identity, encryption, and disaster recovery.

use_cases.title: Compare Workload Placement Options
use_cases.description: Assess where applications should run based on latency, cost, performance, operational maturity, and data gravity.

use_cases.title: Build a Multi-Cloud Cost Strategy
use_cases.description: Create FinOps controls for allocation, chargeback, rightsizing, budget alerts, and cost optimization.

prompt_templates.title: Map My Current Environment
prompt_templates.prompt: Help me document my current hybrid cloud environment. Ask for the minimum inputs needed, then organize the architecture by platforms, networks, identity, data, applications, monitoring, and operational ownership.

prompt_templates.title: Recommend Workload Placement
prompt_templates.prompt: Evaluate workload placement for these applications across AWS, Azure, Google Cloud, OpenStack, VMware, Kubernetes, and edge sites. Consider cost, latency, compliance, data gravity, resilience, and team skills.

prompt_templates.title: Design Hybrid Connectivity
prompt_templates.prompt: Design a redundant hybrid connectivity model for public cloud, private cloud, and edge locations. Include routing, DNS, segmentation, identity boundaries, observability, failover, and operational checks.

prompt_templates.title: Create a Migration Roadmap
prompt_templates.prompt: Create a phased migration roadmap from on-premises systems to a hybrid multi-cloud architecture. Include dependency mapping, landing zones, data migration, rollback plans, security controls, testing, and success metrics.

output_examples.input: Design a hybrid cloud for a financial services workload with strict data residency needs.
output: A target architecture organized by regulated data zones, application tiers, identity, network connectivity, and disaster recovery.
A workload placement table that explains which components stay private and which can run in public cloud.
A control checklist covering encryption, key management, audit logging, monitoring, and compliance evidence.

output_examples.input: Optimize costs for workloads running across OpenStack, AWS, and Azure.
output: A cost review framework for rightsizing, reserved capacity, storage tiering, and chargeback.
A prioritized action plan that separates quick savings from architectural changes.
A governance model with budget alerts, owner tags, anomaly review, and regular optimization reviews.

output_examples.input: Plan disaster recovery across public cloud and on-premises platforms.
output: A recovery design with RTO, RPO, replication approach, failover routing, and runbook ownership.
A testing plan for planned exercises, evidence capture, and post-test improvements.
A list of platform risks that need validation before production use.

best_practices: Provide business goals, compliance requirements, current platforms, network constraints, and workload characteristics before asking for architecture guidance.
Ask for decision records that explain tradeoffs, assumptions, risks, and verification steps.
Validate recommendations against current provider documentation, internal standards, and security review before implementation.
anti_patterns: Do not ask for a single cloud choice without sharing workload, data, compliance, and operational constraints.
Do not treat cost estimates as final quotes without current pricing and usage validation.
Do not implement generated architecture guidance without review by network, security, platform, and compliance owners.

faq.question: What platforms does this skill cover?
faq.answer: It covers AWS, Azure, Google Cloud, OpenStack, VMware, Kubernetes, OpenShift, and common edge computing patterns.

faq.question: Can it create a complete migration plan?
faq.answer: Yes. It can structure phases, dependencies, risks, rollback planning, security controls, and validation steps.

faq.question: Can it deploy infrastructure directly?
faq.answer: No. It provides planning guidance and operational recommendations, but it does not execute cloud deployments.

faq.question: Is it useful for regulated industries?
faq.answer: Yes. It can map architecture decisions to compliance, data residency, identity, encryption, logging, and recovery requirements.

faq.question: Does it include cost optimization?
faq.answer: Yes. It supports FinOps planning, rightsizing, budget controls, chargeback models, and cross-provider cost comparisons.

faq.question: What inputs improve the output quality?
faq.answer: Share workload requirements, current topology, data classifications, latency targets, recovery targets, compliance needs, and team constraints.
